children.value: ) using Huckel theory. First we write the secular determinant using the rules given earlier. To do this, it is convenient to number the carbon atoms in the molecule:

children.html: CH21=CH2−CH3=CH24\overset{1}{\textnormal{CH}_2} = \overset{2}{\textnormal{CH}} - \overset{3}{\textnormal{CH}} = \overset{4}{\textnormal{CH}_2}CH2​1​=CH2−CH3=CH2​4​

children.value: In this case there are two scenarios to consider:

children.html: π\piπ

children.value: electrons are shared either between atoms 1 and 2 or between atoms 3 and 4. This implies that the

children.html: β\betaβ

children.value: parameter should not be written between nuclei 2 and 3.

children.html: π\piπ

children.value: electrons are delocalized over all four carbons. This implies that the

children.html: β\betaβ

children.value: parameter should be written between nuclei 2 and 3.

Here it turns out that scenario 2 gives a lower energy solution, and we study that in more detail. In general, however, both cases should be considered. The energy difference between scenarios 1 and 2 is called the resonance stabilization energy. The secular determinant is:

children.html: 1234∣α−Eβ00βα−Eβ00βα−Eβ00βα−E∣=0{\begin{matrix} 1\\2\\3\\4\\ \end{matrix}\begin{vmatrix} \alpha - E & \beta & 0 & 0\\ \beta & \alpha - E & \beta & 0\\ 0 & \beta & \alpha - E & \beta\\ 0 & 0 & \beta & \alpha - E\\ \end{vmatrix} = 0}1234​∣∣​α−Eβ00​βα−Eβ0​0βα−Eβ​00βα−E​∣∣​=0
